problem: Suppose that we are given 40 points equally spaced around the perimeter of a square, so that four of them are located at the vertices and the remaining points divide each side into ten congruent segments.  If $P$, $Q$, and $R$ are chosen to be any three of these points which are not collinear, then how many different possible positions are there for the centroid of $\triangle PQR$?

classified_questions.question: How many equally spaced points are there around the perimeter of the square?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What are the coordinates of the vertices of the square?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: How many congruent segments divide each side of the square?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you explain why the centroid of triangle PQR must lie in the interior of the square?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: What is the formula for finding the coordinates of the centroid of a triangle?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: Can you explain why the coordinates of the centroid must be of the form (m/3, n/3) where m and n are integers?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many possible positions are there for the centroid of triangle PQR?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you explain why every point of the form (m/3, n/3) could be a centroid?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you identify the cases that divide the points into different possible positions for the centroid?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many possible positions are there for the centroid in each case?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you calculate the total number of possible positions for the centroid using the number of positions in each case?
classified_questions.label: b
